FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study; 10,574 early adolescents aged 9-14 years from 21 recruitment sites across the U.S.

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper only reports TV/screen time as integrated 24-hour movement behavior. Does not isolate smartphone or social media as separate qualifying variables. Abstract mentions 'screen time' but does not report platform-specific, app-category, or social media use separately. Fails B7: unspecified screen time without device/platform distinction.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effects regression models comparing adherence categories to cognitive, psychosocial, and morphometric outcomes; cross-sectional (T1) and longitudinal (T1-T2) analyses reported with regression coefficients.

FT_IC4.evidence: Standardized regression coefficients (β), 95% CIs, and p-values reported in main text (Figure 2, Tables); multiple coefficients for different 24-hour movement combinations.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD Study data with associations between adherence to 24-hour movement guidelines and health outcomes.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published original article; no evidence of duplicate, preprint, or abstract version.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 NOT_MET disqualifies FT-EC7 evaluation. Screen time is not a qualifying smartphone/SM variable per binding rules (B5, B7).
notes: Paper examines 24-hour movement guidelines integrating sleep, physical activity, and screen time. Screen time is measured as total unspecified screen time without platform/device breakdown, failing the smartphone/social media exposure requirement.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study; 10,335 eligible participants at Year 2 (mean age 12); longitudinal follow-up to Year 3 (mean age 13).

FT_IC2.evidence: Cyberbullying alone reported as exposure. Per B3: Cyberbullying without separately-measured SM coefficient = NOT_MET. Paper reports cyberbullying victimization predicting substance use initiation; no separate SM exposure measure or coefficient.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multivariable logistic regression; cross-sectional analysis at Year 2 and longitudinal analysis Year 2 → Year 3; odds ratios (OR) and 95% CIs reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: ORs and 95% CIs reported in Tables 2 (main text): e.g., OR=2.17 [1.68, 2.81] for lifetime cyberbullying; OR=2.22 [1.68, 2.93] for longitudinal association.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD Study data examining association between cyberbullying victimization and substance initiation.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published original article; no evidence of duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: FT-IC2 NOT_MET disqualifies FT-EC7 evaluation. Cyberbullying alone without separately-measured SM fails binding rule B3.
notes: Paper focuses on cyberbullying victimization as primary exposure, not qualifying smartphone or social media variables. Cyberbullying is explicitly excluded as a standalone exposure per B3 unless paired with separately-measured SM having own coefficient.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study; 5,027 adolescents assessed before pandemic (10-13 years) and across six time points May 2020 - March 2021 during pandemic.

FT_IC2.evidence: Social media use (platform-unspecified) and video gaming separately measured and reported. Abstract: 'Higher social media use and video gaming were associated with shorter time in bed, later bedtimes, and longer sleep onset latency.' Qualifies per B1 (separately-measured SM and gaming with own coefficients in mixed-effects models).

FT_IC3.evidence: Mixed-effect models examining associations between self-reported sleep and screen time (social media, video gaming) using longitudinal data; regression coefficients reported.

FT_IC4.evidence: Regression coefficients and statistical test results reported in main text describing associations between social media use, video gaming, and sleep outcomes (time in bed, bedtimes, sleep onset latency).

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis of ABCD Study data examining longitudinal associations between screen time behaviors (social media, video gaming) and sleep outcomes before and during COVID-19 pandemic.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published original article; no evidence of duplicate.

FT_EC7_reason: SM is independent variable predicting sleep outcome (dependent variable), not SM as dependent variable. FT-EC7 requires SM as DV in EVERY analysis; this paper has SM → sleep outcome pattern, failing condition (a).
notes: Paper explicitly reports separate coefficients for social media use and video gaming predicting sleep outcomes. Both cross-sectional (pre-pandemic) and longitudinal (pandemic periods) analyses reported. Meets all FT-IC1-6; FT-EC7 does not apply because SM is predictor of sleep, not outcome.
